D6 Display L4 Analyze Reference

CRT vs LCD: Light in Time

a CRT flashes each phosphor and lets it fade; an LCD holds its light steady — two opposite ways to paint a frame

A CRT scans an electron beam across phosphors that flash bright then decay in under a millisecond, so each point is dark most of the frame — an impulse display that flickers but has crisp motion. An LCD holds a steady backlight behind liquid-crystal shutters: a sample-and-hold display, flicker-free but prone to motion blur.

Watch the light output of one pixel over time. The CRT spikes once per refresh and decays to black; the LCD sits at a flat level the whole frame. Toggle between them to compare the temporal profiles.
